summary: Two similarly-sized enterprise projects (100 KLOC each) have drastically different build times: one takes an hour, the other 2-3 minutes. The key differentiator is database access during testing. The slow project has about 50% of tests hitting the database directly, while the fast project uses an object-relational mapping layer (Neo) that isolates the application from the database. This architectural decision means only the ORM layer tests require database access, with most tests running entirely in-memory.
key_points: Build time differences of 20-30x can exist between similar-sized projects
Database-accessing tests are the primary cause of slow build times
An ORM layer that properly isolates business logic enables fast in-memory testing
Architectural decisions about data access have direct impact on development velocity
money_quotes: One can do a full build and test in an hour, the other takes 2-3 minutes.
Both projects have a big test suite and it's the tests that take the most of the long build time, in particular the tests that access the database.
Only the tests for neo hit the database, most tests are all in-memory and thus much faster.
key_insight: Proper architectural isolation of database access through an ORM layer can reduce build times by an order of magnitude by enabling in-memory testing for the majority of your test suite.
